docs.title: 3psLCCA App
docs.text: Welcome to 3psLCCA, a desktop application for lifecycle cost and carbon assessment workflows.

docs.title: What You Can Do
docs.text:
• Create and manage project-based LCCA studies
  • Enter structural, demolition, recycling, maintenance, and traffic data
  • Track carbon emission and financial impact components
  • Review calculated outputs and visualizations
  • Recover checkpointed work and validate project data

docs.title: Application Modules
docs.text:
The app is organized into major modules under the GUI:

  • Structure and material data
  • Carbon emission analysis
  • Financial data and WPI support
  • Demolition, recycling, and maintenance inputs
  • Output charts and reporting views

docs.title: Documentation Map
docs.text:
• API docs: API Reference Overview
  • User docs: User Guide Overview

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
1. Set up the Python environment with the project dependencies.
  2. Launch the GUI entry point from src/gui/main.py.
  3. Create a new project and populate module inputs.
  4. Save checkpoints and review computed outputs.

Use the navigation tabs above to continue with API and user documentation.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Automatically generated from Python docstrings using mkdocstrings.

docs.title: core.safechunk_engine
docs.text:
SafeChunkEngine v3.0

Project structure

chunks/x.lcca — current save chunks_bak/x.lcca.bak — previous save chunks_bak/x.lcca.ebak — one before that manifest.json — chunk registry + SHA256 hashes version.json — project metadata project_meta.json — author / editor history (always plain JSON) wal.log — crash protection, cleared on clean close checkpoints/ manual/ — user-created checkpoints, last 10 auto/ — auto on clean close if content changed, last 5 .lock — pid lock

docs.title: SafeChunkEngine
docs.text:
SafeChunkEngine(
    project_id: str,
    display_name: str = None,
    app_version: str = "1.0.0",
    debounce_delay: float = 1.0,
    force_save_delay: float = 2.0,
    base_dir: str = "user_projects",
    readable: bool = False,
)

SafeChunk Engine v3.0

  • Auto-saves every 1-2s via debounce + force-save timers
  • Rolling 3-copy backup per chunk (.lcca / .lcca.bak / .lcca.ebak)
  • WAL for crash protection
  • SHA256 integrity check on open
  • Auto-checkpoint on clean close (last 5 kept)
  • readable=True: plain JSON output; readable=False: binary LCCA (default)

docs.title: detach
docs.text:
detach()
Safe close sequence
  1. Force sync all staged data
  2. Cancel timers
  3. Update manifest hashes
  4. Auto-checkpoint if session was dirty
  5. Clear WAL
  6. Write clean version.json
  7. Remove stale tmps (final cleanup)
  8. Release lock

docs.title: stage_update
docs.text:
stage_update(data: dict, chunk_name: str)

Buffers data in memory and triggers debounce + force-save timers. Appends to WAL immediately for crash protection.

docs.title: fetch_chunk
docs.text:
fetch_chunk(chunk_name: str) -> dict

Returns chunk data. Priority: staged memory → .lcca → .lcca.bak → .lcca.ebak → {}

docs.title: force_sync
docs.text:
force_sync()

docs.title: data.generate_wpi_hash
docs.text:
generate_wpi_hashes.py

Loops through every entry in wpi_db.json, recomputes the SHA-256 hash from its data block, and writes the updated file back.

Usage

python generate_wpi_hashes.py # uses wpi_db.json in same dir python generate_wpi_hashes.py path/to/wpi_db.json # explicit path

docs.title: BaseDataWidget
docs.text:
BaseDataWidget(controller=None, chunk_name: str = None)

Bases: QWidget

Base class for all data-entry widgets.

Subclasses only need to
  1. Call super().init(controller=..., chunk_name="...")
  2. Use self.field(key, widget, **options) to create + register each input
  3. Build their layout normally

docs.title: excel_importer.py
docs.text:
Excel → Material import pipeline.

Flow
  1. User picks an .xlsx file via ExcelImportDialog
  2. Parser reads every sheet (sheet name = structural category)
  3. Schema verifier checks columns and flags row-level errors
  4. ImportPreviewWindow opens — one tab per sheet
  5. Invalid cells highlighted red
  6. "Issues" column summarises problems per row
  7. User can edit cells inline before confirming
  8. On confirm → validated dicts printed to console (replace the print() call with your controller / engine write)

docs.title: Column format  (CID# prefix)
docs.text:
Every recognised column must be prefixed with "CID#" (case-insensitive). The part after "cid#" must match the canonical field name EXACTLY.

Valid : CID#Name, cid#name, CID#NAME, Cid#Rate Invalid: CID#Mat_Name, CID#material_name, Name, rate

Canonical field names Required : ID, Name, Quantity, Unit, Rate, Component Optional : Rate_Src, Carbon_Emission_Factor, Carbon_Emission_units, Conversion_Factor, Carbon_Emission_Src, Scrap_Rate, Recovery_Pct, Grade, Type

docs.title: Sheet → chunk routing
docs.text: Sheet names are matched (case-insensitive) against SHEET_TO_CHUNK. Unrecognised sheet names are routed to str_misc and the sheet name is used as the component name (auto-created if it doesn't exist yet).

SQLite-backed store for user-created custom material databases. Multiple logical databases live in a single file, distinguished by the db_name column (e.g. "biharSOR-2026", "My Materials").

Thin wrapper around a single SQLite file that stores custom SOR-style materials. Each 'logical database' the user creates is just a distinct value in the db_name column — no separate files needed.

docs.title: db_registry.py
docs.text: Auto-discovers every SOR JSON file under a configurable root folder (default: material_database/), validates each file's integrity, and writes a single registry manifest (db_registry.json) that downstream tools (search_engine.py, etc.) use to locate and filter databases by region / city.

docs.title: Folder convention expected
docs.text: material_database/ └── / e.g. INDIA └── / e.g. Maharashtra, Delhi └── SOR.json e.g. MumbaiSOR.json

docs.title: Build / refresh the registry manifest
docs.text: python db_registry.py

docs.title: In other modules
docs.text: from db_registry import get_registry, get_path, load, check_integrity

docs.title: check_integrity_by_path
docs.text:
check_integrity_by_path(file_path: str) -> dict

Run a full integrity check on any SOR JSON path (no registry required). Returns a report dict.

docs.title: check_integrity
docs.text:
check_integrity(db_key: str) -> dict

Run integrity check by db_key (requires registry manifest to exist).

docs.title: build_registry
docs.text:
build_registry(
    root: str = MATERIAL_DB_ROOT, manifest_path: str = REGISTRY_MANIFEST_PATH
) -> dict

Crawl root recursively, validate every *.json file, and write manifest_path (db_registry.json).

docs.title: Manifest structure
docs.text:
{ "_meta": { "built_at": "...", "root": "...", "total": N, "ok": N, "failed": N }, "MumbaiSOR": { "db_key": "MumbaiSOR", "path": "/abs/path/to/MumbaiSOR.json", "country": "INDIA", "region": "Maharashtra", "status": "OK" | "FAILED", "record_count": 15, "sheets": ["Foundation", "Sub Structure", ...], ← category list "types": ["Excavation", "Pile", ...], ← type list "errors": [], "warnings": [...], "file_meta": { "size_bytes": ..., "last_modified": ..., "md5": ... } }, ... }

Returns the manifest dict.

docs.title: get_registry
docs.text:
get_registry(manifest_path: str = REGISTRY_MANIFEST_PATH) -> dict

Load and return the registry manifest (minus the _meta key). Auto-builds the registry if the manifest file does not exist.

docs.title: get_path
docs.text:
get_path(db_key: str, manifest_path: str = REGISTRY_MANIFEST_PATH) -> str

Return absolute path for a registered db_key.

docs.title: list_databases
docs.text:
list_databases(country: str = None, region: str = None) -> list[dict]

Return all registered databases, optionally filtered by country / region. Each entry includes db_key, path, country, region, status, sheets, types.

docs.title: load
docs.text:
load(db_key: str, strict: bool = True) -> list[dict]

Integrity-check then return parsed JSON for db_key. Raises RuntimeError on failure when strict=True.

docs.title: search_engine.py
docs.text: Loads material databases from the registry (db_registry.json) and provides category-wise listing and full-text search across one or more regional SOR files.

docs.title: Key public API
docs.text:
engine = MaterialSearchEngine() # loads all OK databases engine = MaterialSearchEngine(region="Maharashtra")# filter by region engine = MaterialSearchEngine(db_keys=["MumbaiSOR"])

engine.list_categories() # all sheetName values engine.list_by_category("Foundation") # all records in category engine.list_by_category("Foundation", "Pile") # filter to type too engine.search("steel rebar") # full-text across all engine.search("steel rebar", category="Foundation") engine.search("PVC", region="Delhi")

docs.title: normalize staticmethod
docs.text:
normalize(text: str) -> str

Lowercase, strip special chars, collapse spaces.

docs.title: tokenize staticmethod
docs.text:
tokenize(text: str) -> list[str]

Split normalized text into word tokens.

docs.title: is_match staticmethod
docs.text:
is_match(query: str, item_name: str) -> bool

True if every query token appears somewhere in the item name. Order-independent, partial-word and concatenated-unit aware.

Examples that all return True ───────────────────────────── query "m35 500 mm" → item "Concrete 500 mm (m35)" query "500mm m35" → item "Concrete 500 mm (m35)" query "concrete m35"→ item "Concrete 500 mm (m35)"

docs.title: MaterialSearchEngine
docs.text:
MaterialSearchEngine(
    db_keys: list[str] | None = None,
    country: str | None = None,
    region: str | None = None,
)

Loads validated SOR databases from the registry and exposes category-wise listing and full-text search.

docs.title: Parameters

docs.title: gui.components.utils.form_builder.form_builder
docs.text: Generic form builder for ScrollableForm subclasses.

docs.title: Core function
docs.text:
build_form(host, fields, base_docs_url) -> list[str]

Iterates a list of Section / FieldDef entries, creates the appropriate Qt widgets, registers them on the host via host.field(), wires up style-reset signals, and returns the list of required field keys.

The host must expose

host.form – QFormLayout to add rows to host.field(key, w) – registers widget and returns it (ScrollableForm API) host._on_field_changed() – called after upload_img interactions

docs.title: build_form
docs.text:
build_form(
    host: Any, fields: list[Section | FieldDef], base_docs_url: str = ""
) -> list[str]

Iterate fields and populate host.form (a QFormLayout).

For each FieldDef: - Creates a labeled section widget with explanation text + optional docs link - Instantiates the correct Qt input widget for the field type - Registers it via host.field(key, widget) and sets it as host.<key> - Wires up style-reset signals - Tracks required keys

For each Section: - Renders a bold header label and a horizontal divider

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: host : ScrollableForm subclass The form instance being populated. fields : list[Section | FieldDef] Declarative field definitions (see form_definitions.py). base_docs_url : str Base URL for documentation links. Field doc_slug is appended. Pass empty string to disable all ⓘ links.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: list[str] Keys of all required fields, ready to assign to host.required_keys.

docs.title: gui.components.utils.form_builder.form_definitions
docs.text: Dataclasses for declarative form field definitions.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from gui.utils.form_definitions import Section, FieldDef

FIELDS = [
    Section("Project Information"),
    FieldDef("project_name", "Project Name", "Official name...",
             "text", required=True, doc_slug="project-name"),

    Section("Project Settings"),
    FieldDef("currency", "Currency", "Currency used...",
             "combo", options=CURRENCIES, required=True, doc_slug="currency"),

    FieldDef("agency_logo", "Agency Logo", "Upload agency logo...",
             "upload_img", options="default", doc_slug="agency-logo"),
]

docs.title: Field types
docs.text:
"text"       – QLineEdit (single line)
"textarea"   – QTextEdit (multi-line)
"int"        – QSpinBox;       options = (min, max)
"float"      – QDoubleSpinBox; options = (min, max, decimals)
"combo"      – QComboBox;      options = [str, ...]
"upload_img" – image picker;   options = preset string or dict
               (see gui.utils.image_utils.resolve_img_settings)

docs.title: Section dataclass
docs.text:
Section(title: str)

A visual section header with a horizontal divider. Not a data field.

docs.title: FieldDef dataclass
docs.text:
FieldDef(
    key: str,
    title: str,
    explanation: str,
    field_type: str,
    options: Any = None,
    unit: str = "",
    required: bool = False,
    doc_slug: str = "",
    warn: tuple | None = None,
    default: Any = None,
)

Declaration of a single form input field.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: key : str Attribute name used for widget registration (self.field(key, widget)). title : str Human-readable label shown above the input. explanation : str Helper text shown below the title. Rendered as rich text with an inline ⓘ docs link when doc_slug is provided. field_type : str One of: "text", "textarea", "int", "float", "combo", "upload_img". options : Any Type-specific configuration: text / textarea – None (unused) int – (min: int, max: int) float – (min: float, max: float, decimals: int) combo – list[str] upload_img – "default" | "no_compression" | dict unit : str Optional unit suffix appended to spinbox widgets, e.g. "(m)". Empty string if not applicable. required : bool Whether the field must be non-empty/non-zero before form submission. doc_slug : str Appended to the base docs URL to form the ⓘ link target. Empty string disables the link.

docs.title: gui.components.utils.form_builder.image_utils
docs.text: Image processing utilities for form upload_img fields. Handles compression preset resolution, settings validation, and image encoding.

docs.title: resolve_img_settings
docs.text:
resolve_img_settings(preset: str | dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any] | None

Resolve an image compression preset to a settings dict.

Accepted values

"default" -> {"max_px": 354, "quality": 85} "no_compression" -> None (caller skips processing) dict -> validated and coerced custom settings

Raises ValueError / TypeError on invalid input.

docs.title: compress_image
docs.text:
compress_image(file_path: str, settings: dict[str, Any] | None) -> bytes

Open, optionally resize/compress, and return image bytes.

If settings is None (no_compression preset) the image is simply read and re-encoded as PNG or JPEG without any size changes.

Settings keys used

max_px – uniform cap on both width and height max_width – cap on width only max_height – cap on height only quality – JPEG quality (1–95); ignored for PNG max_size_bytes – currently informational; not enforced via sweep

docs.title: image_file_to_base64
docs.text:
image_file_to_base64(
    file_path: str, preset: str | dict[str, Any] = "default"
) -> str

Full pipeline: resolve preset -> compress -> base64 encode. Returns a UTF-8 base64 string ready for storage.

docs.title: gui/components/utils/unit_resolver.py
docs.text:
Pure-dict unit analysis — mirrors the logic in material_dialog.py.

No SymPy dependency. All lookups use the same UNIT_TO_SI / UNIT_DIMENSION tables from definitions.py that drive the MaterialDialog UI, so the two subsystems can never diverge.

Public API (backward-compatible): get_unit_info(code, custom_units=None) -> (to_si, dimension) suggest_cf(mat_code, denom_code, ...) -> float | None analyze_conversion_sympy(mat, denom, cf, ...) -> dict (main entry point) validate_cf_simple(mat, denom, cf) -> dict

docs.title: load_custom_units
docs.text:
load_custom_units() -> None

(Re-)load all user-defined custom units from the DB into the cache.

Call this once at app startup and again whenever the user adds/deletes a custom unit via the UI.

docs.title: get_custom_units
docs.text:
get_custom_units() -> list[dict]

Return the current in-process custom-units list.

docs.title: get_known_units
docs.text:
get_known_units() -> set[str]

Return the full set of recognised unit codes (canonical + aliases).

Derived from UNIT_TO_SI and _UNIT_ALIASES so there is a single source of truth — no hardcoded lists elsewhere in the codebase.

docs.title: get_unit_info
docs.text:
get_unit_info(
    code: str, custom_units: list | None = None
) -> tuple[float | None, str | None]

Return (to_si, dimension) for code.

Resolution order
  1. Canonical UNIT_TO_SI / UNIT_DIMENSION registry.
  2. Global custom-units cache (loaded from DB), then the explicit custom_units list if provided (kept for backward compatibility).
  3. _UNIT_ALIASES fallback (then re-tries 1 & 2).

Returns (None, None) when the unit is completely unknown.

docs.title: suggest_cf
docs.text:
suggest_cf(
    mat_code: str, denom_code: str, custom_units: list | None = None
) -> float | None

Return the auto-suggested conversion factor, or None if not determinable.

Logic identical to MaterialDialog._update_cf: • mat == denom → 1.0 (hidden field in the UI) • same dimension → mat_si / denom_si (unit conversion) • different dims → None (user must supply a physical value, e.g. density)

docs.title: analyze_conversion_sympy
docs.text:
analyze_conversion_sympy(
    mat_unit: str,
    carbon_unit_denom: str,
    conv_factor,
    custom_units: list | None = None,
) -> dict

Analyse whether conv_factor is plausible for the given unit pair.

Returns a dict matching the original shape so all existing callers work

{ "kg_factor": float | None, "is_suspicious": bool, "comment": str, "debug_dim_match": bool, }

Suspicion rules mirror MaterialDialog._update_cf / validate_and_accept: 0. CF ≤ 0 → always suspicious. 1. Same unit code → CF should be 1; flag if not. 2. Same dimension, diff unit → expected CF = mat_si/denom_si; flag if >1% off. 3. Material is Mass → kg_factor = mat_si; not suspicious on its own. 4. Denominator is Mass → CF converts qty → mass; flag if CF = 1 (placeholder). 5. Different non-mass dims → flag only if CF = 1 (likely placeholder). 6. Unknown units → string-equality fallback.

docs.title: validate_cf_simple
docs.text:
validate_cf_simple(mat_unit: str, carbon_unit_denom: str, cf: float) -> dict

Quick plausibility check.

Returns {"sus": bool, "suggest": str | None}.

Mirrors MaterialDialog validate_and_accept logic

• CF ≤ 0 → sus, suggest="pos" • same unit → sus if CF ≠ 1, suggest="1" • same dim → sus if CF deviates from mat_si/denom_si, suggest=expected • diff dims → sus if CF = 1, suggest="!1"

docs.title: gui.components.utils.validation_helpers
docs.text:
gui/components/utils/validation_helpers.py

Shared helpers for form validation across all data-entry pages.

docs.title: Usage pattern
docs.text:
Simple page (only build_form() fields): def validate(self): return validate_form(MY_FIELDS, self)

Page with warning thresholds

def validate(self): return validate_form(MY_FIELDS, self, warn_rules=WARNING_RULES)

Page with extra validation (tables, custom widgets, etc.): def validate(self): result = validate_form(MY_FIELDS, self) extra_errors = self._validate_my_table() if extra_errors: result["errors"].extend(extra_errors) return result

Caller checks success as

result = page.validate() if not result["errors"] and not result["warnings"]: # all good

docs.title: Standard validation flow
docs.text:
1. Clear styles — reset all visual state before re-validating
  2. Required checks — is the field filled at all? → result["errors"]
  3. Range/warn checks — is the value plausible? → result["warnings"] (skips fields already in errors) (skips optional fields with no value)
  4. Cross-field checks — handled externally by the caller via the extra-validation pattern
  5. Return — {"errors": [...], "warnings": [...]}
Each layer only runs on fields that passed the previous layer

required=True, empty → error (stops here, warn skipped) required=False, empty → skip (neither error nor warning) required=any, value present, in range → ok required=any, value present, out of range → warning

docs.title: field_title
docs.text:
field_title(key: str, fields: list) -> str

Return the human-readable title for a field key, falling back to the key.

docs.title: clear_field_styles

clear_field_styles(fields: list, widget_owner, skip_keys: set = None)

Clear validation border styles from all FieldDef widgets.

docs.title: validate_form
docs.text:
validate_form(
    fields: list, widget_owner, warn_rules: dict = None, skip_keys: set = None
) -> dict

Standard validation for fields generated by build_form().

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: {"errors": [str, ...], "warnings": [str, ...]} Both lists empty → validation passed. Caller checks: if not result["errors"] and not result["warnings"]

docs.title: set_lock_tooltip_target

set_lock_tooltip_target(widget) -> None

Register the Lock button so the filter can point users to it. Call with the button when locking, None when unlocking.

docs.title: freeze_form
docs.text:
freeze_form(
    fields: list, widget_owner, frozen: bool = True, skip_keys: set = None
) -> None

Make all FieldDef widgets in fields non-editable (frozen=True) or restore them to editable (frozen=False).

Widget behaviour

QLineEdit / QTextEdit → setReadOnly (value stays visible) QAbstractSpinBox → setReadOnly (value stays visible) QComboBox → setEnabled (no readOnly equivalent)

All affected widgets receive the lock tooltip and event filter when frozen so the tooltip fires immediately on any interaction attempt.

docs.title: freeze_widgets
docs.text:
freeze_widgets(frozen: bool, *widgets) -> None

Freeze/unfreeze arbitrary non-FieldDef widgets (buttons, tables, etc.).

When frozen: disables the widget AND installs the lock event filter so the tooltip fires immediately if the user somehow interacts with it. When unfrozen: re-enables and removes the filter.

def freeze(self, frozen: bool = True):
    freeze_form(MY_FIELDS, self, frozen)
    freeze_widgets(frozen, self.btn_clear_all, self.btn_load_suggested)

docs.title: scripts.stamp_hashes
docs.text:
stamp_hashes.py

Run this script at build/release time to compute and stamp SHA-256 hashes into all DB entries in wpi_db.json.

Usage

python stamp_hashes.py python stamp_hashes.py --db path/to/wpi_db.json

docs.title: User Guide
docs.text:
Automatically generated from downloaded markdown sources.

  • Combined Guide

docs.title: Source Pages
docs.text: • 1 Emission Factors Freight Vehicles

docs.title: User Guide
docs.text: Automatically generated from downloaded markdown sources.

docs.title: Definitions
docs.text:
• Vehicle Type Classification of freight vehicles based on their gross vehicle weight (GVW).

  • Gross Weight (GVW) Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW) is the maximum allowable combined mass of the vehicle chassis, transported material (payload), fuel, accessories, and occupants, expressed in tonnes (t).

  • Vehicle Capacity Maximum cargo (payload) the vehicle can carry, in tonnes (t).

  • Empty Vehicle Weight Calculated as: Empty Weight = Gross Weight − Capacity.

  • Emission Factor (kg CO₂/tonne-km) Average carbon dioxide emissions produced per tonne of freight transported over one kilometre.

  • Total Emissions Formula (Round Trip) Assuming the vehicle goes fully loaded to the site and returns empty:

Total Emissions = Number of Trips × (2 × Empty Vehicle Weight + Cargo Capacity) × Distance × Emission Factor
  • Source Reference document from which the emission factors are derived.

docs.title: Emission Factors by Vehicle Category
docs.text: Vehicle Type Gross Weight Range (t) Vehicle Class Emission Factor (kg CO₂/tonne-km) Source Light Duty Vehicle < 4.5 LDV 1.2 Transport. In: Climate Change 2014: Mitigation of Climate Change, IPCC Heavy Duty Vehicle 4.5 – 9 HDV (Small) 0.7 Transport. In: Climate Change 2014: Mitigation of Climate Change, IPCC Heavy Duty Vehicle 9 – 12 HDV (Medium) 0.55 Transport. In: Climate Change 2014: Mitigation of Climate Change, IPCC Heavy Duty Vehicle > 12 HDV (Large) 0.19 Transport. In: Climate Change 2014: Mitigation of Climate Change, IPCC

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
1. Choose the vehicle type and class based on the Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW).
  2. Use the emission factor corresponding to the vehicle type and class for calculating freight emissions.
  3. The formula above assumes a fully loaded outbound trip and an empty return trip, which is standard in logistics carbon accounting.
